docs.title: UCC — Unitary Compiler Collection
docs.text: A multi-level Ahead-of-Time (AOT) compiler and Schrodinger Virtual Machine for quantum circuits.

docs.title: What is UCC?
docs.text:
UCC compiles universal quantum circuits (Clifford + T and beyond) into a compact bytecode representation that is executed by a high-performance virtual machine. It solves the exponential memory wall of non-Clifford simulation by factoring the quantum state into deterministic coordinate transformations and probabilistic complex amplitudes.

\[|\psi\rangle = \gamma \, U_C \, P \, \Big( |\phi\rangle_A \otimes |0\rangle_D \Big)\]

Only the \(2^k\) amplitudes of qubits in active superposition are stored — not \(2^n\) for all \(n\) qubits. For circuits where non-Clifford entanglement is bounded (e.g., magic state distillation), this yields exponential memory savings.

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
import ucc

# Compile a circuit from Stim format
program = ucc.compile("""
    H 0
    CNOT 0 1
    T 2
    M 0 1 2
""")

# Sample measurement outcomes
results = ucc.sample(program, shots=1000)
print(results)  # Array of measurement bitstrings

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• :material-lightning-bolt: AOT Compilation

    Clifford gates are absorbed at compile time. The VM only executes non-Clifford operations and measurements.

  • :material-memory: Factored State

    Memory scales as \(2^k\) where \(k\) is the number of active (non-Clifford) qubits, not \(2^n\) total qubits.

  • :material-speedometer: RISC Bytecode VM

    Cache-aligned 32-byte instructions. Single memory allocation. No dynamic resizing in the hot loop.

  • :material-format-list-checks: Stim Compatible

    Parses Stim circuit format with support for noise channels, detectors, and repeat blocks.

docs.title: Get Started
docs.text: Install UCC Try the Explorer

docs.title: Compiler Explorer
docs.text:
The UCC Compiler Explorer is an interactive browser-based tool for writing, compiling, and simulating quantum circuits. It runs entirely in your browser using WebAssembly — no installation required.

  • :material-open-in-new: Launch Explorer

    Write Stim circuits, see the compiled bytecode, and simulate measurement outcomes — all in your browser.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Monaco editor with Stim syntax highlighting
  • Live compilation showing the RISC bytecode and Heisenberg IR
  • Simulation with configurable shot count and histogram visualization
  • Active qubit history chart showing \(k\) (active dimension) over time
  • Share links for saving and sharing circuits via URL
  • Dark/light theme with system preference detection
  • Guided tour for first-time users

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The explorer compiles the UCC C++ core to WebAssembly using Emscripten. The full AOT compilation pipeline — parsing, front-end Clifford absorption, optimization, back-end virtual compression, and bytecode emission — runs client-side in the browser. Simulation executes the same Schrodinger Virtual Machine as the native build.

Performance

The WebAssembly build is slower than native C++ (roughly 3-5x). For production workloads, use the Python package.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• CMake 3.20+
  • C++ compiler with C++20 support:
    • Linux: GCC 10+ or Clang 12+
    • macOS: Xcode Command Line Tools (Clang 14+) or brew install llvm
  • Python 3.12+
  • uv — curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

docs.title: Python Package Build (Recommended)
docs.text:
Builds the ucc Python package with C++ extensions:

git clone https://github.com/unitaryfoundation/ucc-next.git
cd ucc-next

uv venv
uv pip install -e .

# Verify
uv run python -c "import ucc; print(ucc.version())"

# Run tests
uv run pytest tests/python/ -v

The editable install (-e .) means you can re-run uv pip install -e . after modifying C++ code to rebuild.

docs.title: Standalone C++ Build
docs.text:
For pure C++ development without Python:

cmake -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug
cmake --build build -j
ctest --test-dir build --output-on-failure

docs.title: Build Types
docs.text:
Type Flag Use Case Debug -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug Development (default) Release -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release Benchmarking RelWithDebInfo -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=RelWithDebInfo Profiling

First build takes 10-15 minutes

Stim (a dependency) has many source files. Subsequent builds are incremental. If you hit memory pressure, reduce parallelism: cmake --build build -j1

docs.title: WebAssembly Build
docs.text:
For the browser-based Compiler Explorer:

# Requires Docker
just build-wasm
just test-wasm

Outputs explorer/public/ucc_wasm.{js,wasm}. See the Explorer page.

docs.title: IDE Setup
docs.text:
CMake exports compile_commands.json for IDE integration:

ln -sf build/compile_commands.json .

This enables clangd, VS Code C++ extension, and CLion to provide accurate diagnostics.

docs.title: just Shortcuts
docs.text:
The repository includes a justfile for common tasks:

just --list    # Show all recipes
just py        # Full Python workflow (venv + install + test)
just build     # Build C++ targets
just test      # Run C++ tests
just lint      # Run pre-commit checks

just is optional — all underlying commands are documented above.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: UCC is developed at unitaryfoundation/ucc-next on GitHub.

docs.title: Development Workflow
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository and create a feature branch
  2. Make your changes with atomic, conventional commits (feat:, fix:, test:, docs:)
  3. Run pre-commit checks before committing
  4. Open a pull request against main

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text:
We use pre-commit hooks to enforce formatting and linting:

# Install pre-commit hooks (runs automatically on git commit)
uv run pre-commit install

# Run all checks manually
uv run pre-commit run --all-files

docs.title: C++
docs.text:
• Formatter: clang-format
  • Standard: C++20
  • Namespace: All code in namespace ucc { ... }
  • Comments: Explain why, not what. Omit if the code is self-explanatory.

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
• Linter/Formatter: Ruff
  • Type checker: mypy (strict mode)
  • Python version: 3.12+

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
PythonC++Both 
uv run pytest tests/python/ -v

cmake -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug
cmake --build build -j
ctest --test-dir build --output-on-failure

just py-test
just test

docs.title: Code Coverage
docs.text:
# Python coverage
just py-cov

# C++ coverage (requires lcov)
just cpp-cov

# Both
just cov

HTML reports are generated at coverage/python/index.html and coverage/cpp/index.html.

docs.title: From PyPI
docs.text:
pip install ucc

Python 3.12+ required

UCC requires Python 3.12 or later.

docs.title: Platform Support
docs.text: Platform Status Linux (x86_64) :white_check_mark: Supported macOS (Intel & Apple Silicon) :white_check_mark: Supported Windows :x: Not yet supported

docs.title: From Source
docs.text:
For development or if pre-built wheels aren't available for your platform:

git clone https://github.com/unitaryfoundation/ucc-next.git
cd ucc-next

# Using uv (recommended)
uv venv
uv pip install -e .

# Verify
uv run python -c "import ucc; print(ucc.version())"

First build takes 10-15 minutes

The initial build compiles Stim (a dependency) from source, which has many files. Subsequent incremental builds are fast.

docs.title: Prerequisites (source build)
docs.text:
• CMake 3.20+
  • C++ compiler with C++20 support (GCC 10+, Clang 12+, or Xcode CLT)
  • Python 3.12+
  • uv (recommended) — curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

See Building from Source for the full development setup.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide walks through compiling and simulating your first quantum circuit with UCC.

docs.title: Your First Circuit
docs.text:
UCC uses Stim circuit format as input. Here's a Bell state circuit:

import ucc

circuit = """
    H 0
    CNOT 0 1
    M 0 1
"""

# Compile to bytecode
program = ucc.compile(circuit)

# Sample 1000 shots
results = ucc.sample(program, shots=1000)
print(results)

The output is an array of measurement bitstrings. For a Bell state, you'll see either 00 or 11 with roughly equal probability.

docs.title: Non-Clifford Gates
docs.text:
UCC extends Stim's gate set with non-Clifford gates like T and T_DAG:

import ucc

program = ucc.compile("""
    H 0
    T 0
    H 0
    M 0
""")

results = ucc.sample(program, shots=10000)

# Count outcomes
ones = sum(r[0] for r in results)
print(f"|1> probability: {ones/len(results):.3f}")  # ~0.146

docs.title: Statevector Access
docs.text:
For debugging or verification, you can extract the full statevector:

import ucc

# Compile without measurements
program = ucc.compile("""
    H 0
    CNOT 0 1
""")

# Execute and get the statevector
state = ucc.execute(program)
sv = ucc.get_statevector(state)
print(sv)  # [0.707+0j, 0+0j, 0+0j, 0.707+0j]

docs.title: Noisy Circuits
docs.text:
UCC supports Stim's noise channels for error modeling:

import ucc

program = ucc.compile("""
    H 0
    DEPOLARIZE1(0.01) 0
    CNOT 0 1
    DEPOLARIZE2(0.01) 0 1
    M 0 1
""")

results = ucc.sample(program, shots=10000)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Compiling Circuits — the compilation pipeline in detail
  • Simulation — sampling, statevectors, and detectors
  • Supported Gates — full gate reference

docs.title: Compiling Circuits
docs.text: UCC compiles quantum circuits through a four-stage Ahead-of-Time (AOT) pipeline. This page explains each stage and the Python API for interacting with them.

docs.title: The Compilation Pipeline
docs.text:
Circuit Text --> Parse --> Front-End --> Optimizer --> Back-End --> Program
                 |           |              |            |            |
              Circuit     HirModule     HirModule    Program     Bytecode
               (AST)       (HIR)    (Optimized HIR)  (RISC)     ready to
                                                                 execute

docs.title: One-Step Compilation
docs.text:
For most use cases, ucc.compile() runs the full pipeline:

import ucc

program = ucc.compile("""
    H 0
    CNOT 0 1
    T 2
    M 0 1 2
""")

docs.title: Step-by-Step Compilation
docs.text: You can also run each stage individually for inspection or custom pipelines:

docs.title: 1. Parsing
docs.text:
ucc.parse() converts Stim circuit text into an AST:

import ucc

circuit = ucc.parse("""
    H 0
    CNOT 0 1
    M 0 1
""")

You can also parse from a file:

circuit = ucc.parse_file("my_circuit.stim")

docs.title: 2. Lowering (Front-End + Optimizer + Back-End)
docs.text:
ucc.lower() takes a parsed circuit through the front-end, optimizer, and back-end:

program = ucc.lower(circuit)

docs.title: 3. Custom Pass Managers
docs.text:
The optimizer uses a pass manager to apply transformation passes:

import ucc

# Get the default pass manager
pm = ucc.default_pass_manager()

# Or build a custom one with specific passes
pm = ucc.PassManager()
pm.add(ucc.PeepholeFusionPass())

docs.title: Tracing
docs.text:
ucc.trace() returns a detailed trace of the compilation pipeline for debugging:

import ucc

trace = ucc.trace("""
    H 0
    CNOT 0 1
    T 2
    M 0 1 2
""")
print(trace)

The trace shows the Heisenberg IR, optimized IR, and final bytecode instructions.

docs.title: Compilation Targets
docs.text:
UCC can compile for different measurement output formats using ucc.Target:

import ucc

# Default: measurement bits
program = ucc.compile(circuit_text, target=ucc.Target.MEASUREMENTS)

# Detector + observable format (for QEC)
program = ucc.compile(circuit_text, target=ucc.Target.DETECTORS)

docs.title: Supported Gates
docs.text: UCC parses Stim circuit format and supports nearly all Stim gates, plus non-Clifford extensions.

docs.title: Pauli Gates
docs.text:
Gate Notes I Identity (no-op, parsed but not emitted) X Pauli X Y Pauli Y Z Pauli Z

All Pauli gates are single-qubit Cliffords absorbed at compile time (zero VM cost).

docs.title: Single-Qubit Clifford Gates
docs.text:
Gate Notes H Hadamard (alias: H_XZ) S Phase gate (alias: SQRT_Z) S_DAG Inverse phase gate (alias: SQRT_Z_DAG) SQRT_X, SQRT_X_DAG Square root of X and inverse SQRT_Y, SQRT_Y_DAG Square root of Y and inverse H_XY, H_NXY Hadamard variants in X,Y plane H_YZ, H_NYZ Hadamard variants in Y,Z plane H_NXZ Negated Hadamard C_XYZ, C_ZYX, ... Period-3 Clifford rotations (all 8 variants)

All single-qubit Cliffords are absorbed AOT — they update the Clifford frame \(U_C\) at compile time and have zero cost at runtime.

docs.title: Non-Clifford Gates
docs.text:
Gate Notes T \(\pi/8\) gate T_DAG Inverse \(\pi/8\) gate

These are UCC extensions beyond Stim's gate set. Non-Clifford gates activate qubits in the virtual machine, expanding the active statevector.

docs.title: Two-Qubit Clifford Gates
docs.text:
Gate Notes CX / CNOT / ZCX Controlled-X CY / ZCY Controlled-Y CZ / ZCZ Controlled-Z SWAP Qubit swap ISWAP, ISWAP_DAG Imaginary swap and inverse CXSWAP, SWAPCX CX+SWAP composites CZSWAP / SWAPCZ CZ+SWAP composite SQRT_XX, SQRT_XX_DAG Square root of XX and inverse SQRT_YY, SQRT_YY_DAG Square root of YY and inverse SQRT_ZZ, SQRT_ZZ_DAG Square root of ZZ and inverse XCX, XCY, XCZ X-controlled gates YCX, YCY, YCZ Y-controlled gates

Two-qubit Cliffords are also absorbed at compile time.

docs.title: Measurements and Resets
docs.text: Instruction Notes M / MZ Z-basis measurement MX X-basis measurement MY Y-basis measurement MR / MRZ Measure + reset (Z-basis) MRX Measure + reset (X-basis) MRY Measure + reset (Y-basis) R / RZ Reset to \(\|0\rangle\) RX Reset to \(\|+\rangle\) RY Reset to \(\|{+i}\rangle\)

docs.title: Multi-Qubit Measurements
docs.text: Instruction Notes MPP Multi-Pauli product measurement MXX Pair XX measurement (desugared to MPP) MYY Pair YY measurement (desugared to MPP) MZZ Pair ZZ measurement (desugared to MPP)

docs.title: Noise Channels
docs.text: Instruction Notes DEPOLARIZE1(p) Single-qubit depolarizing noise DEPOLARIZE2(p) Two-qubit depolarizing noise X_ERROR(p) Single-qubit X error Y_ERROR(p) Single-qubit Y error Z_ERROR(p) Single-qubit Z error PAULI_CHANNEL_1(px,py,pz) General single-qubit Pauli channel PAULI_CHANNEL_2(...) General two-qubit Pauli channel (15 params)

docs.title: Annotations and Control Flow
docs.text: Instruction Notes REPEAT N { ... } Loop (unrolled at parse time) DETECTOR QEC detector declaration OBSERVABLE_INCLUDE Observable accumulator MPAD Deterministic measurement padding TICK Timing layer marker QUBIT_COORDS Coordinate annotation (discarded) SHIFT_COORDS Coordinate shift (discarded)

docs.title: Not Yet Supported
docs.text: Gate Category Reason CORRELATED_ERROR / E Noise Correlated multi-qubit error model ELSE_CORRELATED_ERROR Noise Depends on CORRELATED_ERROR HERALDED_ERASE Noise Heralded erasure not modeled HERALDED_PAULI_CHANNEL_1 Noise Heralded channel not modeled SPP, SPP_DAG Pauli product Stochastic Pauli product gate

docs.title: Simulation
docs.text: UCC's Schrodinger Virtual Machine (SVM) executes compiled programs to produce measurement results or statevectors.

docs.title: Sampling
docs.text:
ucc.sample() runs a compiled program for multiple shots and returns measurement outcomes:

import ucc

program = ucc.compile("""
    H 0
    CNOT 0 1
    M 0 1
""")

# Sample 10,000 shots
results = ucc.sample(program, shots=10000)

# results is a 2D array: (shots x num_measurements)
print(results.shape)  # (10000, 2)
print(results[:5])    # First 5 shots

docs.title: Statevector Extraction
docs.text:
For exact state inspection (without measurement collapse):

import ucc

program = ucc.compile("""
    H 0
    CNOT 0 1
""")

state = ucc.execute(program)
sv = ucc.get_statevector(state)

# sv is a numpy array of complex amplitudes
print(sv)  # [0.707+0j, 0+0j, 0+0j, 0.707+0j]

Statevector scales exponentially

get_statevector() expands the factored state into a dense \(2^n\) vector. This is only practical for circuits with a moderate number of qubits. Use max_sim_qubits() to check the configured limit.

docs.title: Post-Selection (Survivor Sampling)
docs.text:
For circuits with post-selection (e.g., magic state distillation), use sample_survivors():

import ucc

program = ucc.compile(circuit_text)

# Only returns shots that pass post-selection
survivors = ucc.sample_survivors(program, shots=1_000_000)
print(f"Survival rate: {len(survivors) / 1_000_000:.4f}")

This is critical for distillation circuits with >99% discard rates — doomed shots are fast-failed immediately, avoiding wasted computation.

docs.title: Detector Sampling
docs.text:
For quantum error correction workflows, compile with the DETECTORS target to get detector and observable outcomes:

import ucc

program = ucc.compile(circuit_text, target=ucc.Target.DETECTORS)
results = ucc.sample(program, shots=10000)
# Each row contains detector bits followed by observable bits

docs.title: Simulation Limits
docs.text:
import ucc

# Check the maximum number of qubits for statevector extraction
print(ucc.max_sim_qubits())  # Default: 25

The SVM can handle circuits with many more physical qubits than this limit — the factored state representation means only \(2^k\) amplitudes are stored, where \(k\) is the peak number of simultaneously active (non-Clifford) qubits.

docs.title: Tutorial: Surface Code Error Suppression
docs.text: This tutorial demonstrates using UCC as a sampling backend for quantum error correction experiments. We reproduce a surface code error suppression plot similar to Stim's getting started notebook, using UCC for circuit compilation and sampling, and PyMatching for minimum-weight perfect matching (MWPM) decoding.

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
The experiment follows four steps:

  1. Generate a rotated surface code memory-Z circuit with stim.Circuit.generated()
  2. Compile & sample detector and observable data with ucc.compile() and ucc.sample()
  3. Decode detector syndromes with PyMatching's MWPM decoder
  4. Compute the logical error rate as the fraction of mispredicted observables

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: pip install ucc stim pymatching matplotlib numpy

docs.title: Full Code
docs.text:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pymatching
import stim

import ucc


def generate_surface_code_circuit(
    distance: int,
    rounds: int,
    phys_error_rate: float,
) -> stim.Circuit:
    """Generate a rotated surface code memory-Z experiment."""
    return stim.Circuit.generated(
        "surface_code:rotated_memory_z",
        rounds=rounds,
        distance=distance,
        after_clifford_depolarization=phys_error_rate,
        after_reset_flip_probability=phys_error_rate,
        before_measure_flip_probability=phys_error_rate,
        before_round_data_depolarization=phys_error_rate,
    )


def estimate_logical_error_rate(
    circuit: stim.Circuit,
    shots: int,
) -> float:
    """Compile with UCC, sample, decode with PyMatching."""
    # UCC compile & sample
    prog = ucc.compile(str(circuit))
    _meas, det, obs = ucc.sample(prog, shots)

    # PyMatching decode
    dem = circuit.detector_error_model(decompose_errors=True)
    matching = pymatching.Matching.from_detector_error_model(dem)
    predictions = matching.decode_batch(det)

    # Logical error = prediction XOR actual observable
    num_errors = int(np.sum(predictions[:, 0] ^ obs[:, 0]))
    return num_errors / shots


# Experiment parameters
distances = [3, 5]
phys_error_rates = [0.001, 0.002, 0.003, 0.005, 0.007, 0.01, 0.015, 0.02, 0.03]
shots = 20_000

# Collect data
results = {}
for d in distances:
    results[d] = []
    for p in phys_error_rates:
        circuit = generate_surface_code_circuit(d, rounds=d, phys_error_rate=p)
        logical_err = estimate_logical_error_rate(circuit, shots)
        results[d].append(logical_err)
        print(f"d={d}  p={p:.4f}  logical_err={logical_err:.5f}")

# Plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
for d in distances:
    ax.plot(phys_error_rates, results[d], "o-", label=f"d = {d}", markersize=7)

ax.plot(phys_error_rates, phys_error_rates, "k--", alpha=0.3, label="logical = physical")
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Physical Error Rate")
ax.set_ylabel("Logical Error Rate")
ax.set_title("Surface Code Error Suppression")
ax.legend()
ax.grid(True, which="both", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("surface_code.png", dpi=150)
plt.show()

docs.title: Circuit Generation
docs.text:
Stim generates the full noisy surface code circuit including:

  • Data qubit initialization and measurement
  • Stabilizer measurement rounds with ancilla qubits
  • Depolarizing noise after Clifford gates, reset, and measurement
  • DETECTOR annotations marking parity checks
  • OBSERVABLE_INCLUDE annotations for logical observable tracking

For distance \(d\), the rotated surface code uses \(d^2 + (d-1)^2\) total qubits. Distance 3 uses 26 qubits; distance 5 uses 64.

docs.title: UCC Compilation & Sampling
docs.text:
ucc.compile() accepts the full Stim circuit text — including REPEAT blocks, noise channels, DETECTOR and OBSERVABLE_INCLUDE annotations. The compiled program is then sampled with ucc.sample(), which returns three arrays:

  • measurements: raw measurement outcomes
  • detectors: detector values (syndrome bits)
  • observables: logical observable outcomes

docs.title: Decoding
docs.text: PyMatching constructs a matching graph from Stim's detector error model and performs MWPM decoding on the detector syndromes. The logical error rate is the fraction of shots where the decoder's prediction disagrees with the actual observable value.

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
The plot shows the classic threshold behavior of the surface code:

  • Below threshold (~\(p = 0.005\)): increasing distance \(d\) exponentially suppresses logical errors. At \(p = 0.001\), distance 5 achieves ~\(10\times\) lower logical error rate than distance 3.
  • Above threshold: larger codes perform worse because they have more opportunities for errors to accumulate.
  • The crossover point is the threshold of the surface code under this noise model.

Current qubit limit

UCC's current MVP supports up to 64 qubits, which accommodates distances 3 (26 qubits) and 5 (64 qubits). Distance 7 would require 118 qubits. Higher distances will be available as UCC's Pauli frame width is expanded.

docs.title: Software Architecture
docs.text: This page describes UCC's concrete software architecture: how the codebase maps to the four-stage pipeline and the key integration contracts.

docs.title: Repository Layout
docs.text:
The source code mirrors the pipeline stages:

Directory Pipeline Stage Role src/ucc/circuit/ Input Circuit AST, parser, target encoding src/ucc/frontend/ Stage 1 Drives stabilizer tableau, absorbs Cliffords, emits HIR src/ucc/optimizer/ Stage 2 Optimization passes (pure HIR manipulation) src/ucc/backend/ Stage 3 Virtual frame tracking, basis compression, bytecode emission src/ucc/svm/ Stage 4 Runtime VM: executes RISC bytecode over dense arrays src/python/ Bindings Python API via nanobind

Isolation Invariant

The VM (svm/) never includes stabilizer tableau code or evaluates tableau mathematics. It executes purely on basic C++ types and arrays.

docs.title: The Stim Integration Contract
docs.text:
UCC uses Stim exclusively as an AOT mathematical tableau library, not as a circuit simulation engine. The runtime VM never touches Stim.

Because UCC factors the state into physical and virtual coordinate frames, the AOT compiler manipulates the stabilizer frame from both ends of the circuit:

docs.title: Front-End: Physical Frame (Prepending)
docs.text:
The Front-End tracks \(U_{\text{phys}}^\dagger\). As it steps forward through the circuit, it prepends physical gates to the inverse tableau:

  • API: sim.inv_state.prepend_H_XZ(), sim.inv_state.prepend_ZCX()
  • Extraction: sim.inv_state.zs[q] extracts the physical \(Z_q\) operator rewound to \(t = 0\)

docs.title: Back-End: Virtual Frame (Appending)
docs.text:
The Back-End synthesizes virtual compression sequences. These are mathematically appended to the tracking frame \(V_{\text{cum}}\):

  • API: Uses Stim's TableauTransposedRaii for efficient row operations
  • Execution: transposed_raii.append_ZCX(), transposed_raii.append_H_XZ()

The final offline Clifford frame for statevector expansion is:

\[U_C = U_{\text{phys}} \, V_{\text{cum}}^\dagger\]

docs.title: Bytecode Format
docs.text:
The VM executes a RISC instruction set with 32-byte cache-aligned instructions. Each instruction encodes:

  • An opcode (gate type, frame operation, measurement, etc.)
  • Up to 2 virtual axis indices (uint16_t)
  • Gate parameters (rotation angles, probabilities)

The fixed instruction size ensures L1 cache alignment and predictable memory access patterns during the hot simulation loop.

docs.title: Memory Model
docs.text:
The VM allocates a single contiguous complex array of size \(2^{k_{\text{max}}}\) at program start. This array is never resized during execution. When measurements reduce the active set, the array is logically compacted (the compiler emits SWAP instructions to route measured qubits to the top axis before measurement).

The Pauli frame (\(P\)) is tracked as a pair of \(n\)-bit integers using Stim's bitword type, supporting efficient SIMD operations.

docs.title: Python Bindings
docs.text:
UCC uses nanobind to expose the C++ core to Python. The Python layer provides:

  • ucc.compile() and ucc.sample() as the primary interface
  • ucc.execute() and ucc.get_statevector() for exact state inspection
  • ucc.trace() for compilation pipeline debugging

See the User Guide for API details.

docs.title: Theoretical Overview
docs.text: UCC (Unitary Compiler Collection) is a multi-level Ahead-of-Time (AOT) compiler and execution engine for universal quantum circuits. It solves the exponential memory wall of non-Clifford simulation by decoupling the deterministic coordinate transformations of a quantum circuit from its probabilistic complex amplitudes.

docs.title: The Factored State Representation
docs.text:
UCC represents the exact physical quantum state at time \(t\) via a strict factorization:

\[|\psi^{(t)}\rangle = \gamma^{(t)} \, U_C^{(t)} \, P^{(t)} \, \Big( |\phi^{(t)}\rangle_A \otimes |0\rangle_D \Big)\]

Each component serves a distinct purpose:

  • \(U_C\) (The Clifford Frame): A static coordinate system mapping virtual qubits to physical qubits. Evaluated entirely at compile time (AOT).

  • \(P\) (The Pauli Frame): Tracks discrete stochastic bit-flips and phase-flips from measurements and noise. Updated at runtime via fast \(\mathcal{O}(1)\) bitwise XOR operations.

  • \(A\) and \(D\) (Active/Dormant Sets): The \(n\) virtual qubits are partitioned into \(k\) Active qubits (in superposition) and \(n - k\) Dormant qubits (in the computational zero state).

  • \(|\phi\rangle_A\) (The Active Statevector): A dense, unnormalized complex array of size \(2^k\) tracking non-Clifford interference. This is the only component that requires exponential memory — but it scales with \(k\), not \(n\).

  • \(\gamma\) (The Global Scalar): Tracks the continuous global phase and physical norm.

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text: For circuits where non-Clifford entanglement is bounded — such as magic state distillation, where syndrome measurements aggressively "cool" superposition — the peak active dimension \(k_{\text{max}}\) remains small even as the total qubit count \(n\) grows to hundreds. UCC allocates \(2^{k_{\text{max}}}\) complex amplitudes instead of \(2^n\), yielding exponential memory savings.

docs.title: The Four-Stage Pipeline
docs.text:
Circuit Text  -->  1. Front-End (Physical Rewinding)
                        |  Absorbs physical Cliffords. Rewinds non-Cliffords
                        |  and measurements to the t=0 vacuum.
                        v
                   Heisenberg IR (HIR)
                        |
                   2. Middle-End (Optimizer)
                        |  O(1) static Pauli commutation checks to fuse
                        |  and cancel redundant operations.
                        v
                   Optimized HIR
                        |
                   3. Back-End (Virtual Compression)
                        |  Maps t=0 Paulis to the active virtual frame.
                        |  Synthesizes greedy O(n) basis compressions.
                        |  Emits localized RISC bytecode.
                        v
                   Program (Bytecode + Constant Pool)
                        |
                   4. Virtual Machine (Execution)
                        |  Executes array updates and tracks Pauli frame P.
                        |  Allocates exactly ONE array of size 2^{k_max}.
                        v
                   Measurement Results

docs.title: Stage 1: Front-End (Physical Rewinding)
docs.text:
The Front-End mathematically absorbs all physical Clifford operations using a stabilizer tableau simulator. For every non-Clifford gate, measurement, or noise channel, it extracts the rewound Pauli string at \(t = 0\):

\[P_{t \to 0} = U_{\text{phys}}^\dagger \, P_t \, U_{\text{phys}}\]

The output is the Heisenberg IR (HIR): a static list of abstract Pauli operations completely devoid of hardware timing or memory layout.

docs.title: Stage 2: Middle-End (Optimizer)
docs.text: The optimizer applies \(\mathcal{O}(1)\) static Pauli commutation checks to fuse and cancel redundant operations in the HIR. Because the HIR is purely algebraic, the optimizer can reason about gate interactions without simulating the full quantum state.

docs.title: Stage 3: Back-End (Virtual Compression)
docs.text:
The Back-End bridges the static HIR to the runtime VM. It maintains a cumulative virtual frame mapping \(V_{\text{cum}}\) and, for each multi-qubit Pauli in the HIR:

  1. Maps the \(t = 0\) Pauli to the current virtual frame: \(P_v = V_{\text{cum}} \, P_{t=0} \, V_{\text{cum}}^\dagger\)
  2. Computes a greedy \(\mathcal{O}(n)\) compression sequence of virtual CNOT/CZ gates to localize \(P_v\) onto a single virtual axis
  3. Emits localized RISC opcodes

Because multi-qubit measurements are explicitly compressed into single-qubit virtual measurements at compile time, no tableau mathematics runs at simulation time.

docs.title: Stage 4: Virtual Machine (SVM)
docs.text:
The VM executes the RISC bytecode over millions of shots. Key properties:

  • Zero-Cost Dormant Operations: If a virtual CNOT targets a dormant qubit (known to be \(|0\rangle\)), the VM simply conjugates the Pauli frame via bitwise XOR. The complex array is untouched.

  • Array Compaction: When an active qubit is measured, it collapses and is demoted to dormant. The compiler emits virtual SWAP instructions to keep the array contiguous in memory, physically halving its size without strided fragmentation.

  • Single Allocation: The VM allocates its complex amplitude array exactly once based on the peak active rank \(k_{\text{max}}\). No dynamic resizing during execution.
